spec_match.rationale: Listens on beforeinput for insertReplacementText. Same problem as the other three concepts in this feature — the v143 change is on the `input` event, not beforeinput; insertReplacementText.dataTransfer was already populated on beforeinput pre-v143.

interactive.rationale: Contenteditable with deliberate misspellings, track-changes toggle, diff inspector, log, restore + clear controls. Real interactivity.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb 'insertReplacementText — for spell-check and autocorrect. Accept a suggestion, see the diff …' — page delivers. But the framing 'the third inputType that grows a dataTransfer' is wrong: this inputType already had a dataTransfer on beforeinput. The growth is on input.

edge_cases.rationale: Uses getTargetRanges() to recover the replaced range — nice touch. Falls back to startContainer.textContent slicing when getTargetRanges is unavailable. Doesn't handle the case where the dataTransfer is missing on the input event (the actual pre-v143 vs v143 contrast), and the track-changes mode doesn't survive a subsequent backspace.

references.rationale: Page contains chromestatus.com/feature/6715253274181632 and the input-events spec.

design_system.rationale: Variables only; .rejected / .accepted styling uses accent colours.

openQuestions.title: Hook the `input` event, not just beforeinput
openQuestions.detail: Spell-check / autocorrect on Chrome 143 fires both beforeinput (pre-v143 already exposed dataTransfer) and input (new in v143). The inspector should log dataTransfer from both events side by side. The most useful demo is: 'on input, dataTransfer was null pre-v143; on v143+ it carries the same payload as beforeinput'.

openQuestions.title: Make track-changes survive subsequent edits
openQuestions.detail: If the user accepts a suggestion, then backspaces, the .rejected / .accepted spans get partially destroyed. Either mark them contenteditable=false (so they delete as units) or attach a MutationObserver that re-wraps. As-is the track-changes mode is fragile.

openQuestions.title: Compare contenteditable vs textarea for spell-check
openQuestions.detail: Spell-check works in textareas too, but the dataTransfer behaviour differs: contenteditable gets the new v143 input-event population, textarea does not. A side-by-side panel would teach the scope limit.

openQuestions.title: Surface an autocorrect-specific test (OS-level)
openQuestions.detail: macOS's autocapitalise / autocorrect also fires insertReplacementText but with no UI cue. A 'try typing 'i think' (lower-case) — macOS will fix it' note would broaden the demo beyond right-click-spelling.

summary: Solid spell-check inspector with track-changes mode but, like the other three concepts, listens on beforeinput rather than input — so it never demonstrates the v143 change. Adding the input-event listener side-by-side is the structural fix shared across all four pages in this feature.
